links.targetTitle: Swarm exposes two primitives: immutable content-addressed /bytes and mutable owner-signed feeds
Manifests are uploaded encrypted to /bytes and only the 64-char hash is written to the feed
The Swarm push cycle serializes, AES-256-GCM encrypts, uploads to /bytes, then writes a feed SOC.

global.title: Encryption lives at the /bytes layer while feeds carry only plain-text content references
global.content:
A load-bearing design rule: encryption lives at the `/bytes` layer, NOT the feed layer. Feeds store plain-text references (content hashes). The data those references point to is what is encrypted.

Consequences:

- Anyone can read the feed and obtain the reference, but without the wallet-derived AES key the `/bytes` payload is opaque.
- Feed-level optimizations (like `uploadReference` vs `uploadPayload`, or batching multiple feed updates) don't affect encryption — they only change how the 64-char hash gets packed into the Single Owner Chunk (SOC), not the encrypted data at `/bytes`.
- The same pattern works for document, drive, and user manifests: serialize JSON, encrypt, upload to `/bytes`, then write only the hash to the feed.

This separation is also why the feed SOC stays tiny (8-byte timestamp + 64-byte reference = 72 bytes) even when manifests grow large.
global.description: Feeds store a 64-char hash pointing at encrypted /bytes data; readers need wallet-derived AES key to decrypt — feed-level optimizations never affect encryption.
